title: Checked and unchecked exception design

canonicalExplanation.summary: Checked exceptions make selected recoverable conditions part of a method's compile-time contract, while unchecked exceptions commonly report programming, invariant, or caller-contract failures.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A file-loading API declares IOException because callers can choose retry, alternate input, or escalation, while an invalid negative argument throws IllegalArgumentException. Your goal is to design and justify exception types and handling policy for a small API. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; String and text processing; Exception flow and stack traces; Classes, fields and methods. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Checked exceptions make selected recoverable conditions part of a method's compile-time contract, while unchecked exceptions commonly report programming, invariant, or caller-contract failures. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: The checked/unchecked choice is an API design decision rather than severity, wrapping everything loses useful distinctions, and exceptions should not encode expected branch flow. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Checked means serious and unchecked means harmless, so every important failure should extend Exception directly.

includes: Choose failure contracts without swallowing errors or using exceptions for normal flow.
Design and justify exception types and handling policy for a small API.
excludes: The checked/unchecked choice is an API design decision rather than severity, wrapping everything loses useful distinctions, and exceptions should not encode expected branch flow.

outcomes: Design and justify exception types and handling policy for a small API.

examples.title: Checked and unchecked exception design: direct language case
examples.situation: A file-loading API declares IOException because callers can choose retry, alternate input, or escalation, while an invalid negative argument throws IllegalArgumentException.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A file-loading API declares IOException because callers can choose retry, alternate input, or escalation, while an invalid negative argument throws IllegalArgumentException.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Choose checked or unchecked from caller recovery and contract needs, not severity; document the failure policy and avoid exceptions for routine alternatives.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Choose checked or unchecked from caller recovery and contract needs, not severity; document the failure policy and avoid exceptions for routine alternatives.

examples.title: Checked and unchecked exception design: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A domain parse method returns an explicit result for common absence but throws a named exception when malformed input prevents a promised conversion.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A domain parse method returns an explicit result for common absence but throws a named exception when malformed input prevents a promised conversion.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: The checked/unchecked choice is an API design decision rather than severity, wrapping everything loses useful distinctions, and exceptions should not encode expected branch flow.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Checked means serious and unchecked means harmless, so every important failure should extend Exception directly.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and justify exception types and handling policy for a small API.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Checked means serious and unchecked means harmless, so every important failure should extend Exception directly.
misconceptions.correction: Choose checked or unchecked from caller recovery and contract needs, not severity; document the failure policy and avoid exceptions for routine alternatives.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Checked means serious and unchecked means harmless, so every important failure should extend Exception directly.

analogy.example: a document desk that records a failure chain, handles only recoverable cases, and always closes borrowed equipment can introduce Checked and unchecked exception design, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: exception boundaries, paths, text encodings, and resource cleanup
limitations: suppressed exceptions, stack unwinding, filesystem semantics, and serialization attacks need exact technical treatment
The checked/unchecked choice is an API design decision rather than severity, wrapping everything loses useful distinctions, and exceptions should not encode expected branch flow.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and justify exception types and handling policy for a small API. Keep this boundary in view: The checked/unchecked choice is an API design decision rather than severity, wrapping everything loses useful distinctions, and exceptions should not encode expected branch flow.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Checked and unchecked exception design using canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate terms and one observable consequence.
Why is this belief incorrect: Checked means serious and unchecked means harmless, so every important failure should extend Exception directly.
Predict this case from memory: A domain parse method returns an explicit result for common absence but throws a named exception when malformed input prevents a promised conversion.
